Dr. Yan Xinqiang is a researcher with a strong background in developing innovative solutions for Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI). Over the course of their career, Dr. Yan's research focus has evolved to encompass multiple areas within MRI technology. Initially, they concentrated on optimizing Radiofrequency (RF) coil design and performance through electromagnetic simulations and experiments, aiming to improve signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and parallel imaging capabilities. This work laid the groundwork for further exploration into decoupling methods for RF arrays, including induced current elimination and magnetic wall decoupling, to minimize electromagnetic coupling between coil elements.

As Dr. Yan's expertise grew, so did their scope of interest. They began investigating high-field MRI techniques, focusing on improving signal-to-noise ratio, mitigating magnetic field inhomogeneities, and optimizing transmit field uniformity in parallel RF transmit design. Additionally, they explored wireless and local receive arrays, designing and evaluating wireless resonator arrays for enhanced receive performance and comparing coupled versus decoupled wireless resonator units. Furthermore, Dr. Yan delved into RF coil design and safety, developing dual-tuned cable traps for multinuclear MRI and MRS, designing self-decoupled transmit/receive coils with improved Specific Absorption Rate (SAR) efficiency, and utilizing baluns to suppress common-mode currents in RF coils. Their diverse range of research interests has positioned them at the forefront of MRI innovation.
